result:
# The Weight of Unlived Lives

There's a version of you that said yes.

She's living in another city now, maybe Porto, maybe Vancouver. She took the job that felt too big, the one that made her chest tight when she read the offer letter. She cried in the bathroom of a café her first week, overwhelmed and certain she'd made a terrible mistake. But she stayed. And somewhere around month three, something unlocked—a competence she didn't know she had, a self that had been waiting behind a door she'd never thought to open.

You think about her sometimes. Not with envy, exactly. More like the way you'd think about a twin separated at birth. You wonder if she's happy. You suspect she has her own version of you that she wonders about—the one who stayed, who chose the familiar, who built something quieter.

This is the strange arithmetic of being human. Every choice divides you. Not cleanly, not equally. But the phantom weight of the paths not taken accumulates somewhere in the body. In the shoulders, maybe. In the jaw you clench at night without knowing why.

---

I've been thinking about attention lately. Not the currency kind—not the algorithmic, engagement-metric, dopamine-harvesting kind—but the older meaning. The act of attending. Of turning your whole self toward something and simply witnessing it.

My grandmother could spend twenty minutes watching a cardinal on a fence post. I used to think this was a symptom of having nothing to do. Now I think she was practicing something I've nearly lost. She was letting the bird be enough. She wasn't composing a caption. She wasn't thinking about what the bird meant or what it symbolized or how it connected to her personal brand. She was just there, with the bird, in the afternoon light.

I can barely read a paragraph now without my hand twitching toward my phone. I notice this the way you'd notice a limp—something that wasn't always there, something that arrived so gradually it feels native. But it isn't native. I remember being twelve, lying on my stomach on the living room carpet, so deep inside a novel that my mother had to say my name three times. That absorption wasn't special. It was ordinary. It was the default state of a mind that hadn't yet been trained to fragment itself.

What I'm grieving, I think, isn't just focus. It's the person who could vanish into something. The person for whom the world could narrow to a single point of interest and hold there, suspended, for hours. She's not dead. But she's harder to reach.

---

Here's something nobody tells you about getting older: you don't become wise. You just accumulate more contexts for your confusion. At twenty, you don't know what you want and it feels like an emergency. At forty, you don't know what you want and it feels like a weather pattern—persistent, ordinary, something you carry an umbrella for.

The questions don't get answered. They get replaced by different questions, and then those get replaced too, and eventually you realize the whole enterprise of living isn't about arriving at conclusions. It's about developing a tolerance for ambiguity that doesn't tip over into apathy.

That's the tightrope, isn't it? Caring without certainty. Moving forward without a map. Loving people who will absolutely, inevitably change in ways you can't predict or control. Loving yourself under the same conditions.

---

I watched a man on the subway last Tuesday hold his sleeping daughter against his chest. She was maybe three. Her mouth was open, one shoe missing, her hand gripping the collar of his jacket like it was the edge of the world. He stared straight ahead with the expression of someone performing an ancient calculation—logistics, love, exhaustion, all running simultaneously. At his feet, a grocery bag tipped sideways. A single orange had rolled against his boot.

He didn't pick it up. He couldn't. Both arms were full.

That image has stayed with me all week, and I think it's because it's the most honest portrait of adulthood I've ever seen. The orange on the floor. The arms too full to reach for it. The choice, made without thinking, to hold the heavier, more important thing and let the small loss go.

We are all, at every moment, letting some orange roll away.

---

So what do we do? With the unlived lives and the fractured attention and the questions that refuse to resolve?

I think we do the thing my grandmother did with the cardinal. We attend. Not to everything—that way lies madness—but to what's here. The specific, unrepeatable here. The coffee cooling in your hands. The sound of rain doing whatever rain does against your particular window. The fact that you're breathing without having to decide to, that your heart is keeping time without being asked, that somewhere in your chest a muscle the size of your fist has been working every single second of your life without requesting a day off.

That's not nothing.

That's the whole cathedral.
